evidence.text: OpenTargets_get_disease_id_description_by_name returned OpenTargets search hits: non-small cell lung carcinoma: A group of at least three distinct histological types of lung cancer, including non-small cell squamous cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, and large cell carcinoma. Non-small cell lung carcinomas have a poor response to conventional chemotherapy.; non-small cell squamous lung carcinoma: A squamous cell carcinoma that arises from the lung. It is characterized by the presence of large malignant cells. It includes the clear cell and papillary variants of squamous cell carcinoma.; lung cancer: A malignant neoplasm involving the lung.

hits.name: non-small cell lung carcinoma
hits.description: A group of at least three distinct histological types of lung cancer, including non-small cell squamous cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, and large cell carcinoma. Non-small cell lung carcinomas have a poor response to conventional chemotherapy.

hits.name: non-small cell squamous lung carcinoma
hits.description: A squamous cell carcinoma that arises from the lung. It is characterized by the presence of large malignant cells. It includes the clear cell and papillary variants of squamous cell carcinoma.

hits.name: lung cancer
hits.description: A malignant neoplasm involving the lung.

hits.name: large cell lung carcinoma
hits.description: A poorly differentiated non-small cell lung carcinoma composed of large polygonal cells without evidence of glandular or squamous differentiation. There is a male predilection.

hits.name: combined small cell lung carcinoma
hits.description: A morphologic variant of small cell lung carcinoma in combination with a non-small cell carcinoma.

hits.name: non-small cell lung adenocarcinoma
hits.description: Type of epithelial lung cancer arising from glandular origin.

hits.name: lung adenocarcinoma
hits.description: A carcinoma that arises from the lung and is characterized by the presence of malignant glandular epithelial cells. There is a male predilection with a male to female ratio of 2:1. Usually lung adenocarcinoma is asymptomatic and is identified through screening studies or as an incidental radiologic finding. If clinical symptoms are present they include shortness of breath, cough, hemoptysis, chest pain, and fever. Tobacco smoke is a known risk factor.

hits.name: Lung Sarcomatoid Carcinoma
hits.description: A rare, aggressive, poorly differentiated, non-small cell lung carcinoma characterized by the presence of a sarcomatoid component often associated with giant cell differentiation. There is a male to female ratio of 4:1. Clinical symptoms include cough, hemoptysis, chest pain, progressive dyspnea and fever secondary to recurrent pneumonia. Cigarette smoking is a major risk factor.

hits.name: methylsuccinate measurement
hits.description: Quantification of the amount of methylsuccinate in a sample.

hits.name: Optic disc pallor
hits.description: A pale yellow discoloration of the optic disc (the area of the optic nerve head in the retina). The optic disc normally has a pinkish hue with a central yellowish depression.

hits.name: cone-rod synaptic disorder syndrome, congenital nonprogressive
GCGR-related hyperglucagonemia
hits.description: A rare tumor of pancreas caused by mutations in the GCGR gene characterized by pancreatic alpha cell hyperplasia, pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors and markedly increased serum glucagon levels in the absence of a glucagonoma syndrome. Clinical manifestations may include abdominal pain, pancreatitis, fatigue, diarrhea, and diabetes mellitus.

hits.name: e3 ubiquitin-protein ligase RNF43 measurement
hits.description: The determination of the amount of e3 ubiquitin-protein ligase RNF43 in a sample

hits.name: night blindness
hits.description: Inability to see clearly in dim light.

hits.name: Hyperhidrosis
hits.description: Abnormal excessive perspiration (sweating) despite the lack of appropriate stimuli like hot and humid weather.

hits.name: disease progression measurement
hits.description: quantification of some aspect of disease progression such as speed or symptom variability. Disease progression can be quantified in a number of ways, for example as a correlated measure of progression scores from different symptom areas

hits.name: pituitary hormone deficiency, combined, 6
hits.description: Any combined pituitary hormone deficiencies, genetic form in which the cause of the disease is a mutation in the OTX2 gene.

hits.name: adult-onset non-insulinoma persistent hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia
hits.description: Diazoxide-resistant hyperinsulism (DRH) is form of congenital isolated hyperinsulinism caused by an abnormal insulin production by b-cells in the pancreas that can be diffuse or focal and is characterized by an excessive/ uncontrolled insulin secretion (inappropriate for the level of glycemia), recurrent episodes of profound hypoglycemia and resistance to medical management with diazoxide

hits.name: diabetes, deafness, developmental delay, and short stature syndrome
hypochromic anemia
hits.description: Anemia caused by the reduction of hemoglobin in relation to the red cell volume. As a result, the red cells have an area of central pallor which is increased in size. The leading cause is iron deficiency.

hits.name: benign soft tissue neoplasm
hits.description: A non-metastasizing neoplasm that arises from the soft tissue.

hits.name: level of aldehyde dehydrogenase X, mitochondrial in blood serum
hits.description: The amount of a aldehyde dehydrogenase X, mitochondrial when measured in blood serum.

hits.name: leucine-induced hypoglycemia
hits.description: Rare cause of hypoglycemia and is described as a condition in which symptomatic hypoglycemia is provoked by high protein feedings. Hypoglycemia is also elicited by administration of oral or intravenous infusions of a single amino acid, leucine. [http://www.uniprot.org/diseases/DI-01896]

hits.name: isolated growth hormone deficiency type IA
hits.description: An isolated growth hormone deficiency characterized by autosomal recessive inheritance of severe dwarfism with onset by 6 months of age and variable development of antibodies to growth hormone following exogenous supplementation that has material basis in null mutations in the GH1 gene on chromosome 17q23.3.

scientific_assessment: Treat as an established or clinically precedented target-disease context; focus on residual mechanism, responder biology, resistance, safety, and patient selection.
The disease-target rationale is biologically plausible when live/public evidence links KRAS to non-small cell lung cancer through disease association, pathway, or mechanism records.
The current claim should remain pathway-level: evidence supports target and mechanism grounding, not clinical efficacy for any intervention unless direct clinical evidence is cited.

Relevant clinical literature titles include: Advances on drug therapy for KRAS(G12C)-mutant non-small-cell lung cancer.; PDK1 and YAP1/TEAD signaling drive acquired KRAS inhibitor resistance in KRAS-mutant non-small cell lung cancer.; Clinical Utility of Circulating Tumor DNA in Patients With Advanced KRAS(G12C)-Mutated NSCLC Treated With Sotorasib..
Candidate molecules or interventions are prioritization leads only; potency, selectivity, exposure, safety, and disease-model response must be tested.

case_profile.objective: Generate a comprehensive mechanistic and translational hypothesis dossier for KRAS G12C pathway inhibition in NSCLC (non-small cell lung cancer). Synthesize: (1) the established clinical precedence for covalent KRAS G12C inhibitors including sotorasib and adagrasib, distinguishing approved indications from unresolved questions about response durability, resistance, and patient selection; (2) primary resistance mechanisms driven by co-mutations in STK11, KEAP1, and SMAD4, and acquired resistance via secondary KRAS mutations, RTK bypass signaling through EGFR and MET upregulation, and RAS pathway reactivation through SOS1 or RAF alterations; (3) rational combination strategies targeting the bypass vulnerabilities — SOS1 inhibition, MEK inhibition, immunotherapy co-administration — and the evidence basis for each; (4) patient stratification biomarkers that predict benefit versus resistance; and (5) ranked next validation experiments that would most change the clinical development strategy, distinguishing experiments that address target validity from those addressing resistance, safety, and patient-selection gaps.

scientist_positions.position: KRAS G12C is a clinically validated target in NSCLC with FDA-approved agents (sotorasib, adagrasib); the field's open questions are resistance biology, combination optimization, and patient selection — not target validity.
key_claims: OpenTargets KRAS-NSCLC association score 0.842 and two approved covalent inhibitors confirm established clinical precedence, not a novel target hypothesis

Primary resistance co-mutations (STK11, KEAP1, SMAD4) and acquired bypass mechanisms (EGFR/MET RTK upregulation, secondary KRAS mutations, SOS1/RAF alterations) are the dominant unresolved translational gaps
Rational combinations (SOS1i, MEKi, PD-1/L1 checkpoint) have mechanistic rationale but lack phase 3 durability data; ctDNA dynamics show early promise as a pharmacodynamic biomarker
Highest-priority validation experiments should address acquired resistance circuitry and predictive biomarker qualification, not target-disease linkage
supporting_evidence_sources: OpenTargets KRAS-NSCLC score 0.842; EGFR 0.888 contextualizes KRAS as tier-1 driver
PubMed PMIDs 42031346, 41668807 — expanding KRAS therapeutic landscape and bench-to-bedside review
PMID 40445863 — ctDNA dynamics predict sotorasib efficacy, supporting liquid biopsy patient selection
PMID 41338446 — SRC as resistance node; PMID 42124568 — mSWI/SNF inhibition re-sensitizes KRAS-mutant tumors
concerns: Median PFS with sotorasib/adagrasib monotherapy remains ~6 months; durability gap is not yet closed by any approved combination
STK11/KEAP1 co-mutation prevalence (~20-30% each) substantially limits monotherapy benefit but prospective biomarker-stratified trials are still maturing
Immunotherapy combinations carry hepatotoxicity signal (sotorasib + pembrolizumab grade 3+ liver toxicity); safety data must gate combination development
SOS1 and MEK inhibitor combination data are predominantly preclinical or early phase; extrapolation to survival benefit is premature
requested_followups: Prospective biomarker-stratified trial co-enrolling STK11/KEAP1 status to quantify differential benefit and identify salvage strategies for co-mutant patients
Randomized phase 2 of KRAS G12Ci + SOS1i vs monotherapy with mandatory on-treatment biopsy to map resistance emergence kinetics
Systematic ctDNA clearance threshold validation as an early pharmacodynamic endpoint across sotorasib and adagrasib cohorts
Mechanistic dissection of PDK1/YAP1-TEAD adaptive resistance (per hypothesis card citation) to determine whether vertical co-inhibition is synthetically lethal or toxic

scientist_positions.position: The hypothesis card accurately frames KRAS G12C inhibition in NSCLC as an established clinical precedent requiring mechanistic and resistance resolution, not a new target discovery. PubMed literature strongly supports this framing.
key_claims: Sotorasib and adagrasib have FDA approval in KRAS G12C NSCLC with documented but limited response durability
STK11, KEAP1, and SMAD4 co-mutations are primary resistance biomarkers with clinical-grade evidence
Acquired resistance via secondary KRAS mutations and RTK bypass (EGFR, MET) is well-documented in published cohorts
Combination strategies (SOS1i, MEKi, immunotherapy) have preclinical and early clinical evidence but lack phase 3 validation
ctDNA dynamics predict sotorasib efficacy per PMID 40445863
supporting_evidence_sources: PMID 42031346: pan-RAS landscape review in KRAS-mutant NSCLC
PMID 41668807: bench-to-bedside therapeutic strategies review
PMID 41338446: SRC and KRAS inhibitor resistance mechanisms
PMID 39828218: sotorasib plus chemotherapy phase 2 (SCARLET)
concerns: No phase 3 head-to-head data yet for combination strategies; early-phase evidence may not translate
STK11/KEAP1 co-mutation resistance evidence is largely retrospective and observational
SOS1 inhibitor combination data are predominantly preclinical; clinical validation is pending
Immunotherapy co-administration safety signal with adagrasib (hepatotoxicity) is underweighted in current card
requested_followups: Prospective biomarker-stratified trial in STK11/KEAP1 co-mutant patients to test alternative combinations
Phase 2 randomized data for SOS1 inhibitor plus KRAS G12C inhibitor in NSCLC
Systematic ctDNA resistance profiling at progression across sotorasib and adagrasib cohorts
Safety and efficacy data for adagrasib plus PD-1 inhibitor from ongoing CodeBreaK and KRYSTAL trials

scientist_positions.position: KRAS G12C is a clinically validated oncogenic driver in NSCLC with FDA-approved covalent inhibitors (sotorasib, adagrasib); the scientific frontier is resistance biology, combination strategy, and patient selection—not target discovery
key_claims: Sotorasib and adagrasib demonstrate proof-of-concept but median PFS ~6 months signals durable resistance as the central unresolved problem
Co-mutations STK11/KEAP1 mechanistically suppress immune surveillance and reduce RAS-pathway dependency, predicting primary resistance
Acquired resistance converges on KRAS secondary mutations (Y96D, R68S), RTK bypass via EGFR/MET, and SOS1-mediated RAS reactivation
Rational combinations (SOS1i, MEKi, anti-PD-1) have mechanistic rationale but lack randomized efficacy data distinguishing additive from synergistic benefit
ctDNA dynamics and co-mutation profiling are the most actionable near-term patient-selection biomarkers
supporting_evidence_sources: PubMed PMID 41668807 therapeutic strategies KRAS G12C bench to bedside
PubMed PMID 41338446 SRC at crossroads of KRAS inhibitor resistance
PubMed PMID 40445863 ctDNA dynamic variation predicts sotorasib efficacy
OpenTargets KRAS association score 0.842 for NSCLC
concerns: STK11/KEAP1 co-mutation prevalence (~20-30%) limits generalizability of single-agent response data across unselected KRAS G12C populations
MEK inhibitor combinations carry overlapping toxicity profiles that may constrain therapeutic window in clinical practice
Immunotherapy co-administration evidence is largely retrospective or small cohort; randomized data are absent
Secondary KRAS mutation heterogeneity (multiple resistance alleles) may preclude single salvage strategy
requested_followups: Prospective randomized trial of sotorasib/adagrasib plus SOS1 inhibitor versus monotherapy stratified by STK11/KEAP1 status
Paired pre/post-treatment tumor biopsies with single-cell multi-omics to map clonal resistance evolution
Functional CRISPR screen in STK11-null KRAS G12C models to identify synthetic lethal co-targets
ctDNA minimal residual disease kinetics as early surrogate endpoint in phase 2 combination trials
scientist_positions.discipline: disease mechanism and causal pathway biology

scientist_positions.position: KRAS G12C is a fully validated, FDA-approved target in NSCLC with sotorasib and adagrasib as precedent agents. The molecule-level case is strong but durability, resistance bypass, and patient-selection gaps remain the central unresolved problems requiring mechanistic and translational work.
key_claims: Sotorasib and adagrasib are covalent GDP-state binders at C12 with distinct pharmacokinetic and CNS-penetrance profiles; structural differences matter for resistance mutation cross-sensitivity
Co-mutations STK11/KEAP1 create primary resistance via immune exclusion and oxidative-stress tolerance, not direct KRAS pathway bypass, requiring orthogonal combination strategies
Acquired resistance converges on RAS-ERK reactivation through secondary KRAS mutations (Y96D, R68S), SOS1/RAF amplification, and RTK upregulation (EGFR, MET, FGFR1), all restoring GTP-loading
SOS1 inhibitors (BI-3406 class) and SHP2 inhibitors address upstream reactivation but lack monotherapy efficacy; MEK inhibition is mechanistically rational but toxicity-limited in combination
supporting_evidence_sources: PubMed PMIDs 42031346, 41668807: pan-RAS landscape and bench-to-bedside reviews confirming resistance mechanism taxonomy
PubMed PMID 41338446: SRC as resistance node downstream of RTK bypass, adding a non-canonical resistance axis
PubMed PMID 40445863: ctDNA dynamics as early pharmacodynamic biomarker for sotorasib efficacy
OpenTargets KRAS-NSCLC association score 0.842, second only to EGFR, confirming target-disease grounding
concerns: No head-to-head resistance-mechanism frequency data across sotorasib vs adagrasib in matched NSCLC cohorts; resistance taxonomy may be drug-specific, not class-wide
STK11/KEAP1 co-mutation resistance evidence is largely retrospective and correlative; causal mechanistic proof in isogenic NSCLC models is incomplete
SOS1 inhibitor combination data are predominantly preclinical; clinical PK/PD confirmation of target engagement in NSCLC patients is absent from cited evidence
PDK1/YAP1-TEAD resistance axis (cited in hypothesis card) is mechanistically distinct from RAS-ERK reactivation and is underweighted in current combination strategies
requested_followups: Prospective ctDNA resistance-mechanism profiling at progression on sotorasib vs adagrasib in matched NSCLC cohorts to establish drug-specific vs class-wide resistance frequencies
Isogenic STK11/KEAP1 knock-in KRAS G12C NSCLC cell line panel to causally dissect immune-exclusion vs cell-autonomous resistance contributions
Phase 1b dose-escalation of SOS1 inhibitor + adagrasib with mandatory on-treatment tumor biopsies for RAS-GTP loading and ERK phosphorylation as PD endpoints
CRISPR dropout screen in KRAS G12C NSCLC lines under adagrasib pressure to rank YAP1/TEAD, PDK1, and SRC as actionable co-dependencies versus epiphenomena

scientist_positions.position: The hypothesis correctly frames KRAS G12C inhibition as clinically precedented in NSCLC, not a novel target discovery. However, several efficacy and safety claims embedded in the broader dossier require challenge: median PFS with sotorasib/adagrasib monotherapy remains modest (~6 months), ORR ~35-43%, and durable responses are the exception rather than the rule. Combination strategies and biomarker-guided selection remain investigational without phase 3 validation. The dossier risks overstating the translational readiness of combination approaches and understating the safety complexity of MEK inhibitor co-administration.
key_claims: Sotorasib and adagrasib have regulatory approval but monotherapy benefit is limited in duration and magnitude, not a solved clinical problem
STK11/KEAP1 co-mutations confer primary resistance with strong retrospective evidence but prospective biomarker-stratified trial data are absent
SOS1 and MEK inhibitor combinations show preclinical/early-phase signals but no phase 3 efficacy or safety data exist to support clinical readiness claims
ctDNA as a predictive biomarker for sotorasib response has emerging but not yet validated prospective evidence
supporting_evidence_sources: PubMed PMIDs 41668807, 42031346: therapeutic strategies and expanding landscape reviews confirm approved indications and resistance biology
PubMed PMID 40445863: ctDNA dynamics correlate with sotorasib efficacy but study is observational, not prospectively validated
PubMed PMID 39828218 (SCARLET trial): sotorasib plus chemotherapy phase 2, single-arm, no randomized comparator — efficacy claims must be qualified
OpenTargets KRAS association score 0.842 for NSCLC confirms strong disease-target linkage but does not validate combination or biomarker strategies
concerns: MEK inhibitor combinations carry substantial overlapping toxicity (rash, diarrhea, hepatotoxicity) not adequately addressed in the dossier's combination rationale

Immunotherapy co-administration with KRAS G12C inhibitors lacks randomized evidence; sotorasib+pembrolizumab showed excess hepatotoxicity in CodeBreaK 101, a critical safety signal being underweighted
STK11/KEAP1 as resistance biomarkers are retrospectively derived; prospective stratification trials are absent, risking premature clinical operationalization

Secondary KRAS mutations (Y96D, H95) as acquired resistance mechanisms are characterized in small datasets; their prevalence and clinical actionability are overstated without larger prospective ctDNA series
requested_followups: Prospective randomized biomarker-stratified trial (STK11/KEAP1 wild-type vs. mutant) to validate primary resistance prediction before clinical use
Systematic safety review of sotorasib/adagrasib plus immunotherapy combinations, specifically hepatotoxicity incidence and grade 3+ event rates across all available trials
Phase 2 randomized data for SOS1 inhibitor combinations (e.g., BI-3406 + MEK) with pre-specified resistance endpoints before phase 3 investment
Prospective ctDNA monitoring study with landmark analysis to establish validated thresholds for early treatment discontinuation or escalation

scientist_positions.position: The hypothesis card correctly frames KRAS G12C inhibition in NSCLC as a clinically precedented target requiring mechanistic and translational refinement, not a novel discovery. However, several claims within the synthesized dossier contain weak evidentiary links, missing controls, and translational gaps that require explicit flagging before this framework can guide clinical development decisions.
key_claims: Co-mutations in STK11/KEAP1/SMAD4 are primary resistance drivers, but causal mechanistic evidence in humans (vs. correlative genomic data) remains sparse and confounded by co-occurrence patterns

Combination strategies (SOS1i, MEKi, immunotherapy) are rationalized from preclinical bypass signaling data, but clinical proof-of-concept for each is at early or absent stages with unresolved toxicity profiles
ctDNA dynamics are proposed as predictive biomarkers, but prospective validation with pre-specified cutoffs and independent cohorts is lacking for most claims

Acquired resistance via secondary KRAS mutations and RTK upregulation is well-documented in cell lines and small clinical series, but the relative frequency and temporal dynamics in unselected NSCLC patients are poorly characterized
supporting_evidence_sources: PubMed PMIDs 42031346, 41668807 provide recent clinical landscape reviews supporting approved indications and resistance taxonomy
OpenTargets KRAS association score 0.842 for NSCLC confirms strong disease-target linkage from multi-evidence aggregation
PMID 40445863 supports ctDNA dynamic variation as a sotorasib efficacy correlate, though single-arm and retrospective
PMID 41338446 identifies SRC as a resistance node, extending beyond canonical RTK/RAS bypass but lacking in vivo combination validation
concerns: STK11/KEAP1 co-mutation resistance claims rely heavily on retrospective subgroup analyses with small N; no randomized stratification by these biomarkers exists, making causal inference premature

MEK inhibitor combinations showed dose-limiting toxicity in early trials (e.g., sotorasib + trametinib); the dossier must not present MEKi combinations as straightforwardly rational without flagging this safety liability

Immunotherapy co-administration evidence is contradictory: STK11-mutant tumors are immunologically cold yet KRAS G12C tumors may have neoantigen burden; conflating these without subgroup resolution risks misleading patient-selection claims

The ranked next-validation experiments section risks circularity if resistance mechanism experiments use the same cell line models that generated the original resistance hypotheses, without orthogonal in vivo or patient-derived organoid confirmation
requested_followups: Prospective biomarker-stratified trial or basket sub-study with pre-specified STK11/KEAP1 co-mutation arms to establish causal resistance contribution versus prognostic confounding
Independent patient-derived organoid or PDX cohort to validate SOS1i and MEKi combination efficacy and therapeutic index before further clinical escalation

Longitudinal paired biopsy study (baseline + progression) in ≥100 sotorasib/adagrasib-treated NSCLC patients to establish relative frequency of each acquired resistance mechanism in an unselected population
Prospective ctDNA landmark study with pre-specified week-4 clearance threshold and overall survival endpoint to validate ctDNA as a decision-making biomarker rather than exploratory correlate

scientist_positions.position: The omics and pathway evidence strongly supports the established mechanistic framework for KRAS G12C inhibition in NSCLC, with well-characterized resistance biology and rational combination targets, but several pathway-level gaps remain unresolved.
key_claims: KRAS G12C covalent inhibitors (sotorasib, adagrasib) have validated clinical precedence with FDA approval in NSCLC
Primary resistance via STK11/KEAP1/SMAD4 co-mutations is mechanistically coherent and supported by pathway data
Acquired resistance through RTK bypass (EGFR, MET), SOS1 reactivation, and secondary KRAS mutations is well-evidenced at the omics level
YAP1/TEAD and PDK1 signaling represent emerging adaptive resistance nodes with preclinical but limited clinical validation
ctDNA dynamics show early promise as a pharmacodynamic and resistance biomarker
supporting_evidence_sources: OpenTargets KRAS-NSCLC association score 0.842 confirms strong disease-target link
PubMed PMIDs 42031346, 41668807 document expanding resistance mechanism landscape
PubMed PMID 40445863 supports ctDNA as sotorasib efficacy predictor
PubMed PMID 41338446 identifies SRC as a novel resistance node at pathway crossroads
concerns: YAP1/TEAD and PDK1 resistance evidence is largely preclinical; clinical validation is lacking
SOS1 inhibitor combination data in NSCLC patients remains sparse and immature
STK11/KEAP1 co-mutation resistance is correlative; direct causal pathway mechanism is not fully resolved
Immunotherapy combination rationale is mechanistically plausible but clinical benefit signal is inconsistent
requested_followups: Prospective co-mutation profiling (STK11, KEAP1, SMAD4) in sotorasib/adagrasib trials to establish causal resistance hierarchy
Paired pre/post-treatment biopsies with multi-omic profiling to distinguish adaptive versus acquired resistance mechanisms
Clinical-grade SOS1 inhibitor combination trial with ctDNA monitoring as primary pharmacodynamic endpoint
Functional CRISPR screens in patient-derived NSCLC organoids to rank resistance node dependencies

agreements: Sotorasib and adagrasib are FDA-approved covalent KRAS G12C inhibitors in NSCLC; target validity is established, not hypothetical
Median PFS ~6 months with monotherapy identifies resistance as the central unresolved translational problem
STK11, KEAP1, and SMAD4 co-mutations are primary resistance biomarkers with clinical-grade but largely retrospective evidence
Acquired resistance via secondary KRAS mutations and RTK bypass (EGFR, MET) is well-documented across published cohorts
ctDNA dynamics show early promise as a pharmacodynamic and patient-selection biomarker (PMID 40445863)
Combination strategies (SOS1i, MEKi, checkpoint inhibition) have mechanistic rationale but lack phase 3 randomized efficacy data
disagreements: Relative weighting of hepatotoxicity risk for sotorasib+pembrolizumab versus adagrasib+PD-1 combinations is inconsistently emphasized across positions
Whether MEK inhibitor combinations are primarily constrained by overlapping toxicity or by lack of efficacy data is not resolved between agents
Degree to which PDK1/YAP1-TEAD adaptive resistance (cited in hypothesis card) represents a generalizable versus context-specific mechanism is unresolved
overclaims: Framing ctDNA clearance thresholds as 'actionable near-term biomarkers' overstates current validation; prospective threshold qualification is still pending
Describing SOS1 inhibitor combinations as having 'early clinical evidence' conflates predominantly preclinical data with clinical validation

Asserting STK11/KEAP1 co-mutations 'mechanistically suppress immune surveillance and reduce RAS-pathway dependency' as established fact overstates causal inference from largely correlative cohort data
required_revisions: Distinguish retrospective/observational STK11/KEAP1 resistance evidence from prospectively validated biomarker data; label confidence level accordingly
Separate sotorasib and adagrasib immunotherapy combination safety profiles; hepatotoxicity signal differs by agent and partner and must not be aggregated
Qualify SOS1i and MEKi combination claims with explicit phase designation (preclinical vs. phase 1/2) and note absence of randomized comparator arms

Add explicit ranking of next validation experiments by decision impact: (1) prospective biomarker-stratified resistance trial, (2) randomized SOS1i+KRAS G12Ci phase 2 with on-treatment biopsy, (3) ctDNA threshold validation, (4) PDK1/YAP1-TEAD synthetic lethality mechanistic dissection

key_claims: Sotorasib and adagrasib are FDA-approved covalent KRAS G12C inhibitors with demonstrated clinical activity in advanced NSCLC, establishing KRAS G12C as a clinically validated therapeutic target in this disease.
Acquired resistance to KRAS G12C inhibition in NSCLC is mechanistically driven by PDK1 activation and YAP1/TEAD signaling, as identified in preclinical and translational resistance studies.
Circulating tumor DNA dynamic variation is associated with sotorasib efficacy in KRAS G12C-mutated advanced NSCLC, supporting ctDNA as a pharmacodynamic biomarker for this therapeutic class.

KRAS holds an Open Targets association score of 0.842 for non-small cell lung carcinoma, ranking it among the top disease-associated targets alongside EGFR (0.888) and ALK (0.812), consistent with its established oncogenic driver role.

critique.critique: The hypothesis card correctly frames KRAS/NSCLC as clinically precedented rather than a new discovery, and FDA-approved agents (sotorasib, adagrasib) confirm target-disease grounding. However, three ClinicalTrials.gov evidence links (KRAS G12C pathway inhibition queries) remain unsupported in the claim graph, creating unresolved evidentiary gaps. STK11 co-mutation evidence is classified as tier_unknown with weak_support, yet is invoked as a patient-selection rationale without mechanistic bridging evidence. The OpenTargets association score of 0.842 for KRAS is presented alongside 13,431 total targets without contextualizing specificity. Safety data from openFDA (adagrasib, sotorasib, trametinib) are flagged as safety_concern but no specific liability thresholds or contraindication claims are made, leaving safety characterization incomplete. Fifteen of 34 evidence items are labeled irrelevant, suggesting evidence curation quality is low.
critique.recommended_fix: 1) Resolve or explicitly disclaim the three unsupported ClinicalTrials.gov claim links. 2) Upgrade STK11 evidence tier with direct mechanistic citations linking STK11 loss to KRAS inhibitor response in NSCLC. 3) Contextualize OpenTargets score with disease-specificity rank. 4) Summarize key openFDA safety signals (e.g., hepatotoxicity for sotorasib, QTc for adagrasib) with severity grades. 5) Remove or quarantine the 15 irrelevant evidence items from the scored pool.
critique.claim_boundary: Hypothesis is appropriately bounded to clinical-precedence review and mechanism/resistance/safety/patient-selection refinement; it does not overclaim novelty. Boundary holds provided the unsupported ClinicalTrials.gov links are resolved and STK11 patient-selection claims are not extended beyond available evidence.

evidence_hierarchy.interpretation: stronger evidence mix with clinical/human support

content.critique: The hypothesis card correctly frames KRAS/NSCLC as clinically precedented rather than a new discovery, and FDA-approved agents (sotorasib, adagrasib) confirm target-disease grounding. However, three ClinicalTrials.gov evidence links (KRAS G12C pathway inhibition queries) remain unsupported in the claim graph, creating unresolved evidentiary gaps. STK11 co-mutation evidence is classified as tier_unknown with weak_support, yet is invoked as a patient-selection rationale without mechanistic bridging evidence. The OpenTargets association score of 0.842 for KRAS is presented alongside 13,431 total targets without contextualizing specificity. Safety data from openFDA (adagrasib, sotorasib, trametinib) are flagged as safety_concern but no specific liability thresholds or contraindication claims are made, leaving safety characterization incomplete. Fifteen of 34 evidence items are labeled irrelevant, suggesting evidence curation quality is low.
content.recommended_fix: 1) Resolve or explicitly disclaim the three unsupported ClinicalTrials.gov claim links. 2) Upgrade STK11 evidence tier with direct mechanistic citations linking STK11 loss to KRAS inhibitor response in NSCLC. 3) Contextualize OpenTargets score with disease-specificity rank. 4) Summarize key openFDA safety signals (e.g., hepatotoxicity for sotorasib, QTc for adagrasib) with severity grades. 5) Remove or quarantine the 15 irrelevant evidence items from the scored pool.
content.claim_boundary: Hypothesis is appropriately bounded to clinical-precedence review and mechanism/resistance/safety/patient-selection refinement; it does not overclaim novelty. Boundary holds provided the unsupported ClinicalTrials.gov links are resolved and STK11 patient-selection claims are not extended beyond available evidence.

case_profile.objective: Generate a comprehensive mechanistic and translational hypothesis dossier for KRAS G12C pathway inhibition in NSCLC (non-small cell lung cancer). Synthesize: (1) the established clinical precedence for covalent KRAS G12C inhibitors including sotorasib and adagrasib, distinguishing approved indications from unresolved questions about response durability, resistance, and patient selection; (2) primary resistance mechanisms driven by co-mutations in STK11, KEAP1, and SMAD4, and acquired resistance via secondary KRAS mutations, RTK bypass signaling through EGFR and MET upregulation, and RAS pathway reactivation through SOS1 or RAF alterations; (3) rational combination strategies targeting the bypass vulnerabilities — SOS1 inhibition, MEK inhibition, immunotherapy co-administration — and the evidence basis for each; (4) patient stratification biomarkers that predict benefit versus resistance; and (5) ranked next validation experiments that would most change the clinical development strategy, distinguishing experiments that address target validity from those addressing resistance, safety, and patient-selection gaps.

key_claims: Sotorasib and adagrasib are FDA-approved covalent KRAS G12C inhibitors with demonstrated clinical activity in advanced NSCLC, establishing KRAS G12C as a clinically validated therapeutic target in this disease.
Acquired resistance to KRAS G12C inhibition in NSCLC is mechanistically driven by PDK1 activation and YAP1/TEAD signaling, as identified in preclinical and translational resistance studies.
Circulating tumor DNA dynamic variation is associated with sotorasib efficacy in KRAS G12C-mutated advanced NSCLC, supporting ctDNA as a pharmacodynamic biomarker for this therapeutic class.

KRAS holds an Open Targets association score of 0.842 for non-small cell lung carcinoma, ranking it among the top disease-associated targets alongside EGFR (0.888) and ALK (0.812), consistent with its established oncogenic driver role.
guardrails: Target-disease or clinical-precedence evidence must be separated from efficacy and safety claims.
Requires experimental validation before clinical interpretation.
